author: John Chrysostom
book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

ccel.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

title: Matthew XV. 1.

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Then came to Jesus Scribes and Pharisees, which were of Jerusalem, saying, Why do Thy disciples,” etc.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then; when? when He had wrought His countless miracles; when He had healed the infirm by the touch of the hem of His garment.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For even with this intent doth the evangelist mark the time, that He might signify their unspeakable wickedness, by nothing repressed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But what means, “The Scribes and Pharisees, which were of Jerusalem?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: In every one of the tribes were they scattered abroad, and divided into twelve parts; but they who occupied the chief city were worse than the others, as both enjoying more honor, and having contracted much haughtiness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But mark, I pray thee, how even by the question itself they are convicted; in not saying, “Why do they transgress the law of Moses,” but, “the tradition of the elders.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Whence it is evident that the priests were inventing many novelties, although Moses, with much terror and with much threatening, had enjoined neither to add nor take away.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For ye shall not add,” saith he, “unto the word which I command you this day, and ye shall not take away from it.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But not the less were they innovating; as in this instance, that one ought not to eat with unwashen hands, that we must wash cups and brazen vessels, that we must wash also ourselves.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus, when men were henceforth, as time advanced, to be freed from their observances, at that very time they bound them with the same in more and more instances, fearing lest any one should take away their power, and wishing to strike more dread, as though they were themselves also lawgivers.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: The thing in fact proceeded so far in enormity, that while their own commandments were kept, those of God were transgressed; and they so far prevailed, that the matter had actually become a ground of accusation.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Which was a twofold charge against them, in that they both invented novelties, and were so strict exactors on their own account, while of God they made no reckoning.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And omitting to speak of the other things, the pots and the brazen vessels (for it was too ridiculous), what seemed more reasonable than the rest, that they bring forward, wishing, as seems at least to me, in that way to provoke Him to anger.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore also they made mention of the elders, in order that He, as setting them at nought, might give occasion against Himself.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But it were meet first to inquire, why the disciples ate with unwashen hands.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore then did they so eat?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Not as making a point of it, but as overlooking henceforth the things that are superfluous, and attending to such as are necessary; having no law to wash or not to wash, but doing either as it happened.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For they that despised even their own necessary food, how were they to hold these things worth much consideration?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This then having often happened unintentionally,—for instance, when they ate in the wilderness, when they plucked the ears of corn,—is now put forward as a charge by these persons, who are always transgressing in the great things, and making much account of the superfluous.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then saith Christ?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He did not set Himself against it, neither made He any defense, but straightway blames them again, plucking down their confidence, and signifying that he who commits great sins ought not to be strict with others concerning small matters.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “What? when you ought to be blamed,” saith He, “do ye even blame?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But do thou observe, how when it is His will to set aside any of the things enjoined by the law, He does it in the form of an apology; and so He did in that case.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For by no means doth He proceed at once to transgress it, nor doth He say, “It is nothing;” for surely He would have made them more audacious; but first He clean cuts away their boldness, bringing forward the far heavier charge, and directing it upon their head.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And He neither saith, “they do well in transgressing it,” lest He should give them a hold on Him; nor doth He speak ill of their proceeding, lest He should confirm the law: nor again, on the other hand, doth He blame the elders, as lawless and unholy men; for doubtless they would have shunned Him as a reviler and injurious: but all these things He gives up, and proceeds another way.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And He seems indeed to be rebuking the persons themselves who had come to Him, but He is reprehending them that enacted these laws; nowhere indeed making mention of the elders, but by His charge against the Scribes casting down them also, and signifying that their sin is twofold, first in disobeying God, next in doing so on men’s account; as though He had said, “Why this, this hath ruined you, your obeying the elders in all things.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yet He saith not so, but this is just what He intimates, by answering them as follows:

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Why do ye also transgress the commandment of God byyour tradition?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For God commanded, saying, Honor thy father and thy mother: and, He that curseth father or mother, let him die the death.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But ye say, Whosoever shall say to his father or his mother, It is a gift, by whatsoever thou mightest be profited by me, andhonor not his father or his mother—And ye have made void the commandmentof God by your tradition.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And He said not, “the elders’ tradition,” but “your own.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And, “ye say;” again He said not, “the elders say:” in order to make His speech less galling.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: That is, because they wanted to prove the disciples transgressors of the law, He signifies that they themselves are doing so, but that these are free from blame.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For of course that is not a law, which is enjoined by men (wherefore also He calls it “a tradition”), and especially by men that are transgressors of the law.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And since this had no shade of contrariety to the law, to command men to wash their hands, He brings forward another tradition, which is opposed to the law.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what He saith is like this.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “They taught the young, under the garb of piety, to despise their fathers.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: How, and in what way?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “If one of their parents said to his child, Give me this sheep that thou hast, or this calf, or any such thing, they used to say, ‘This is a gift to God, whereby thou wouldest be profited by me, and thou canst not have it.’

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And two evils hence arose: on the one hand they did not bring them to God, on the other they defrauded their parents under the name of the offering, alike insulting their parents for God’s sake, and God for their parents’ sake.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But He doth not say this at once, but first rehearses the law, by which He signifies His earnest desire that parents should be honored.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For, “honor,” saith He, “thy father and thy mother, that thou mayest live long upon the earth.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And again, “He that curseth father or mother, let him die the death.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But He, omitting the first, the reward appointed for them that honor their parents, states that which is more awful, the punishment, I mean, threatened to such as dishonor them; desiring both to dismay them, and to conciliate such as have understanding; and He implies them to be for this worthy of death.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if he who dishonors them in word is punished, much more ye, who do so in deed, and who not only dishonor, but also teach it to others.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Ye then who ought not so much as to live, how find ye fault with the disciples?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “And what wonder is it, if ye offer such insults to me, who am as yet unknown, when even to the Father ye are found doing the like?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For everywhere He both asserts and implies, that from Him they began with this their arrogance.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But some do also otherwise interpret, “It is a gift, by whatsoever thou mightest be profited by me;” that is, I owe thee no honor, but it is a free gift from me to thee, if indeed I do honor thee.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But Christ would not have mentioned an insult of that sort.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And Mark again makes this plainer, by saying, “It is Corban, by whatsoever thou mightest be profited by me;”which means, not a gift and present, but properly an offering.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Having then signified that they who were trampling on the law could not be justly entitled to blame men for transgressing a command of certain elders, He points out this same thing again from the prophet likewise.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus, having once laid hold of them severely, He proceeds further: as on every occasion He doth, bringing forward the Scriptures, and so evincing Himself to be in accordance with God.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what saith the prophet?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “This people honoreth me with their lips, but their heart is far from me.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But in vain do they worship me, teaching for doctrines the commandments of men.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou a prophecy in exact accordance with His sayings, and from the very first proclaiming beforehand their wickedness?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For what Christ laid to their charge now, of this Isaiah also spake from the very first; that the words of God they despise, “for in vain do they worship me,” saith He; but of their own they make much account, “teaching,” saith He, “for doctrines the commandments of men.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore with reason the disciples keep them not.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Having, you see, given them their mortal blow; and from the facts first, then from their own suffrage, then from the prophet having aggravated the charge, with them indeed He discourses not at all, incorrigibly disposed as they are now come to be, but directs His speech to the multitudes, so as to introduce His doctrine, great and high, and full of much strictness; and taking occasion from the former topic, He proceeds to insert that which is greater, casting out also the observance of meats.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But see when.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: When He had cleansed the leper, when He had repealed the Sabbath, when He had shown Himself King of earth and sea, when He had made laws, when He had remitted sins, when He had raised dead men, when He had afforded them many proofs of His Godhead, then He discourses of meats.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For indeed all the religion of the Jews is comprised in this; if thou take this away, thou hast even taken away all.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For hereby He signifies, that circumcision too must be abrogated.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But of Himself He doth not prominently introduce this (forasmuch as that was older than the other commandments, and had higher estimation), but He enacts it by His disciples.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For so great a thing was it, that even the disciples after so long a time being minded to do it away, first practise it, and so put it down.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But see how He introduces His law: how “He called the multitude, and said unto them, Hear and understand.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus He doth by no means simply reveal it to them, but by respect and courtesy, first, He makes His saying acceptable (for this the evangelist declares by saying, “He called them unto Him”): and secondly, by the time also; in that after their refutation, and His victory over them, and the accusation by the prophet, then He begins His legislation, when they too would more easily receive His sayings.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And He doth not merely call them unto Him, but also makes them more attentive.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For “understand,” saith He, that is, “consider, rouse yourselves; for of that sort is the law now about to be enacted.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if they set aside the law, even unseasonably, for their own tradition, and ye hearkened; much more ought ye to hearken unto me, who at the proper season am leading you unto a higher rule of self restraint.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And He did not say, “The observance of meats is nothing, neither that Moses had given wrong injunctions, nor that of condescension He did so;” but in the way of admonition and counsel, and taking His testimony from the nature of the things, He saith: “Not the things that go into the mouth, defile the man, but the things that go out of the mouth;”resorting to nature herself both in His enactment and in His demonstration.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yet they hearing all this, made no reply, neither did they say, “What sayest Thou?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: When God hath given charges without number concerning the observance of meats, dost thou make such laws?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But since He had utterly stopped their mouths, not by refuting them only, but also by publishing their craft, and exposing what was done by them in secret, and revealing the secrets of their mind; their mouths were stopped, and so they went away.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But mark, I pray thee, how He doth not yet venture distinctly to set Himself with boldness against the meats.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore neither did He say “the meats,” but, “the things that enter in defile not the man;” which it was natural for them to suspect concerning the unwashen hands also.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For He indeed was speaking of meats, but it would be understood of these matters too.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why, so strong was the feeling of scruple about the meats, that even after the resurrection Peter said, “Not so, Lord, for I have never eaten anything common or unclean.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For although it was for the sake of others that He said this, and in order to leave Himself a justification against his censurers, by pointing out that he actually remonstrated, and not even so was excused, nevertheless it implies the depth of their impression on that point.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore you see He Himself also at the beginning spake not openly concerning meats, but, “The things that go into the mouth;” and again, when He had seemed afterwards to speak more plainly, He veiled it by His conclusion, saying, “But to eat with unwashen hands defileth not the man:”that He might seem to have had His occasion from thence, and to be still discoursing of the same.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore He said not, “To eat meats defileth not a man,” but is as though He were speaking on that other topic; that they may have nothing to say against it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: When therefore they had heard these things, “the Pharisees,” it is said, “were offended,”not the multitudes.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For “His disciples,” so it is said, “came and said unto Him, Knowest thou that the Pharisees were offended, when they heard the saying?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yet surely nothing had been said unto them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then saith Christ?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He did not remove the offense in respect of them, but reproved them, saying, “Every plant which my heavenly Father hath not planted, shall be rooted up.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For He is wont both to despise offenses, and not to despise them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Elsewhere, for example, He saith, “But lest we should offend them, cast an hook into the sea:”but here He saith, “Let them alone, they be blind leaders of the blind: and if the blind lead the blind, both shall fall into the ditch.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But these things His disciples said, not as grieving for those men only, but as being themselves also slightly perplexed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But because they durst not say so in their own person, they would fain learn it by their telling Him of others.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And as to its being so, hear how after this the ardent and ever-forward Peter came to Him, and saith, “Declare unto us this parable,”discovering the trouble in his soul, and not indeed venturing to say openly, “I am offended,” but requiring that by His interpretation he should be freed from his perplexity; wherefore also he was reproved.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then saith Christ?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Every plant which my heavenly Father hath not planted, shall be rooted up.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This, they that are diseased with the Manichæan pest affirm to be spoken of the law; but their months are stopped by what had been said before.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if He was speaking of the law, how doth He further back defend it, and fight for it, saying, “Why do ye transgress the commandments of God for your tradition?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And how doth He bring for ward the prophet?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But of themselves and of their traditions He so speaks.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if God said, “Honor thy father and thy mother,” how is not that of God’s planting, which was spoken by God?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what follows also indicates, that of themselves it was said, and of their traditions.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus He added, “They are blind leaders of the blind.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Whereas, had He spoken it of the law, He would have said, “It is a blind leader of the blind.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But not so did He speak, but, “They are blind leaders of the blind:” freeing it from the blame, and bringing it all round upon them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then to sever the people also from them, as being on the point of falling into a pit by their means, He saith, “If the blind lead the blind, both shall fall into the ditch.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: It is a great evil merely to be blind, but to be in such a case and have none to lead him, nay, to occupy the place of a guide, is a double and triple ground of censure.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if it be a dangerous thing for the blind man not to have a guide, much more so that he should even desire to be guide to another.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then saith Peter?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He saith not, “What can this be which Thou hast said?” but as though it were full of obscurity, he puts his question.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And he saith not, “Why hast thou spoken contrary to the law?” for he was afraid, lest he should be thought to have taken offense, but asserts it to be obscure.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: However, that it was not obscure, but that he was offended, is manifest, for it had nothing of obscurity.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore also He rebukes him, saying, “Are ye also yet without understanding?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as to the multitude, they did not perhaps so much as understand the saying; but themselves were the persons offended.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore, whereas at first, as though asking in behalf of the Pharisees, they were desirous to be told; when they heard Him denouncing a great threat, and saying, “Every plant, which my heavenly Father hath not planted, shall be rooted up,” and, “They are blind leaders of the blind,” they were silenced.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But he, always ardent, not even so endures to hold his peace, but saith, “Declare unto us this parable.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then saith Christ?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: With a sharp rebuke He answers, “Are ye also yet without understanding?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Do ye not yet understand?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But these things He said, and reproved them, in order to cast out their prejudice; He stopped not however at this, but adds other things also, saying, “That whatsoever entereth in at the mouth goeth into the belly, and is cast out into the draught; but those things which proceed out of the mouth come forth from the heart, and they defile the man.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For out of the heart proceed evil thoughts, murders, adulteries, fornications, thefts, blasphemies, false-witnessings: and these are the things that defile the man: but to eat with unwashen hands defileth not the man.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou how sharply He deals with them, and in the way of rebuke?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then He establishes His saying by our common nature, and with a view to their cure.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For when He saith, “It goeth into the belly, and is cast out into the draught,” he is still answering according to the low views of the Jews.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For He saith, “it abides not, but goes out:” and what if it abode? it would not make one unclean.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But not yet were they able to hear this.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And one may remark, that because of this the lawgiver allows just so much time, as it may be remaining within one, but when it is gone forth, no longer.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For instance, at evening He bids you wash yourself, and so be clean; measuring the time of the digestion, and of the excretion.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But the things of the heart, He saith, abide within, and when they are gone forth they defile, and not when abiding only.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And first He puts our evil thoughts, a kind of thing which belonged to the Jews; and not as yet doth He make His refutation from the nature of the things, but from the manner of production from the belly and the heart respectively, and from the fact that the one sort remains, the other not; the one entering in from without, and departing again outwards, while the others are bredwithin, and having gone forth they defile, and then more so, when they are gone forth.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because they were not yet able, as I said, to be taught these things with all due strictness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But Mark saith, that “cleansing the meats,” He spake this.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He did not however express it, nor at all say, “but to eat such and such meats defileth not the man,” for neither could they endure to be told it by Him thus distinctly.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And accordingly His conclusion was, “But to eat with unwashen hands defileth not the man.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us learn then what are the things that defile the man; let us learn, and let us flee them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For even in the church we see such a custom prevailing amongst the generality, and men giving diligence to come in clean garments, and to have their hands washed; but how to present a clean soul to God, they make no account.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And this I say, not forbidding them to wash hands or mouth; but willing men so to wash as is meet, not with water only, but instead of water, with all virtues.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the filth of the mouth is evil speaking, blasphemy, reviling, angry words, filthy talking, laughter, jesting: if then thou art conscious to thyself of uttering none of them, neither of being defiled with this filth, draw near with confidence; but if thou hast times out of number received these stains, why dost thou labor in vain, washing thy tongue indeed with water, but bearing about on it such deadly and hurtful filth?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For tell me, hadst thou dung on thy hands, and mire, wouldest thou indeed venture to pray?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: By no means.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And yet this were no hurt; but that is ruin.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: How then art thou reverential in the different things, but in the forbidden remiss?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then? should not we pray? saith one.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: We should indeed, but not while defiled, and having upon us mire of that sort.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “What then, if I have been overtaken?” saith one.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Cleanse thyself.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “How, and in what way?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Weep, groan, give alms, apologize to him that is affronted, reconcile him to thyself hereby, wipe clean thy tongue, lest thou provoke God more grievously.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For so if one had filled his hands with dung, and then should lay hold of thy feet, entreating thee, far from hearing him, thou wouldest rather spurn him with thy foot; how then durst thou in such sort draw nigh to God?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since in truth the tongue is the hand of them that pray, and by it we lay hold on the knees of God.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Defile it not therefore, lest to thee also He say, “Though ye make many prayers, I will not hearken.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, and “in the power of the tongue are death and life;”and, “By thy words thou shalt be justified, and by thy words thou shalt be condemned.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: I bid thee then watch thy tongue more than the apple of thine eye.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: The tongue is a royal steed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: If then thou put a bridle on it, and teach it to pace orderly, the King will rest and take His seat thereon; but if thou suffer it to rush about unbridled and leap wantonly, it becomes a beast for the devil and bad spirits to ride on.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And while thou, fresh from the company of thine own wife, darest not pray, although this is no blame at all; dost thou lift up thine hands, fresh from reviling and insult, which brings after it no less than hell, before thou hast well cleansed thyself?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And how dost thou not shudder? tell me.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Hast thou not heard Paul, saying, “Marriage is honorable, and the bed undefiled?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if on rising from the undefiled bed, thou darest not draw nigh in prayer, how dost thou coming from the bed of the devil call on that awful and terrible name?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For it is truly the devil’s bed, to wallow in insults and reviling.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And like some wicked adulterer, wrath dallies with us in great delight, casting into us deadly seed, and making us give birth to diabolical enmity, and doing all things in a way opposite to marriage.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For whereas marriage causes the two to become one flesh, wrath severs into many parts them that were united, and cleaves and cuts in pieces the very soul.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: That thou mayest therefore with confidence draw nigh to God, receive not wrath, when it comes in upon thee, and desires to be with thee, but drive it away like a mad dog.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For so Paul too commanded: his phrase being, “lifting up holy hands without wrath and disputing.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Dishonor not then thy tongue, for how will it entreat for thee, when it hath lost its proper confidence? but adorn it with gentleness, with humility, make it worthy of the God who is entreated, fill it with blessing, with much almsdoing.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For it is possible even with words to do alms.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For a word is a better thing than a gift,”and “answer the poor man peaceably with meekness.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And all the rest of thy time too adorn it with the rehearsing of the laws of God; “Yea, let all thy communication be in the law of the Most High.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Having thus adorned ourselves, let us come to our King, and fall at His knees,not with the body only, but also with the mind.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us consider whom we are approaching, and on whose behalf, and what we would accomplish.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: We are drawing nigh unto God, whom the seraphim behold and turn away their faces, not bearing His brightness; at sight of whom the earth trembles.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: We draw nigh unto God, “who dwelleth in the light, which no man can approach unto.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And we draw nigh unto Him for deliverance from hell, for remission of sins, for escape from those in tolerable punishments, for attaining to the Heavens, and to the good things that are there.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us, I say, fall down before Him both in body and in mind, that He may raise us up when we are down; let us converse with all gentleness and meekness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And who is so wretched and miserable, one may say, as not to become gentle in prayer?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He that prays with an imprecation, and fills himself with wrath, and cries out against his enemies.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay, if thou wilt accuse, accuse thyself.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: If thou wilt whet and sharpen thy tongue, let it be against thine own sins.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And tell not what evil another hath done to thee, but what thou hast done to thyself; for this is most truly an evil; since no other will really be able to injure thee, unless thou injure thyself.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore, if thou desire to be against them that wrong thee, approach as against thyself first; there is no one to hinder; since by coming into court against another, thou hast but the greater injury to go away with.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what injury at all hast thou really to mention?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: That such an one insulted and spoiled thee by violence, and encompassed thee with dangers?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay, this is receiving not injury, but if we be sober, the very greatest benefit; the injured being he that did such things, not he that suffered them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And this is more than any one thing the cause of all our evils, that we do not so much as know at all who is the injured, and who the injurious person.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since if we knew this well, we should not ever injure ourselves, we should not pray against another, having learnt that it is impossible to suffer ill of another.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For not to be spoiled, but to spoil, is an evil.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore, if thou hast spoiled, accuse thyself; but if thou hast been spoiled, rather pray for him that spoiled thee, because he hath done thee the greatest good.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For although the intent of the doer was not such, yet thou hast received the greatest benefit, if thou hast endured it nobly.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For him, both men, and the laws of God declare to be wretched, but thee, the injured party, they crown, and proclaim thy praise.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For so if any one sick of a fever had violently taken from any other a vessel containing water, and had had his fill of his pernicious desire, we should not say that the despoiled had been injured, but the spoiler; for he has aggravated his fever, and made his disease more grievous.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now in this way I bid thee reason concerning him also that loves wealth and money.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For he too, having a far worse fever than the other, has by this rapine fanned the flame in himself.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Again, were some madman to snatch a sword from any one, and destroy himself, which again is the injured?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He that hath been robbed, or the robber?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: It is quite clear, he that did the robbery.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Well then, in the case of seizing property also, let us give the same suffrage.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For what a sword is to a madman, much the same is wealth to a covetous man; nay, it is even a worse thing.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the madman, when he has taken the sword, and thrust it through himself, is both delivered from his madness, and hath no second blow to receive; but the lover of money receives daily ten thousand wounds more grievous than his, without delivering himself from his madness, but aggravating it more exceedingly: and the more wounds he receives, the more doth he give occasion for other more grievous blows.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Reflecting then on these things, let us flee this sword; let us flee the madness; though late, let us become temperate.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this virtue too ought to be called temperance, not less than that which is used to be so called among all men.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For whereas there the dominion of one lust is to be struggled against, here we have to master many lusts, and those of all kinds.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, nothing, nothing is more foolishthan the slave of wealth.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He thinks he overcomes when he is overcome.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He thinks he is master, when he is a slave, and putting bonds on himself, he rejoices; making the wild beast fiercer, he is pleased; and becoming a captive, he prides himself, and leaps for joy; and seeing a dog rabid and flying at his soul, when he ought to bind him and weaken him by hunger, he actually supplies him with abundance of food, that he may leap upon him more fiercely, and be more formidable.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Reflecting then on all these things, let us loose the bonds, let us slay the monster, let us drive away the disease, let us cast out this madness; that we may enjoy a calm and pure health, and having with much pleasure sailed into the serene haven, may attain unto the eternal blessings; unto which may we all attain, by the grace and love towards man of our Lord Jesus Christ, to whom be glory and might, now and always, and world without end.
